channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Some tropical butterfly species have unlocked an evolutionary way to live longer - CNN
item.description:
1. Some tropical butterfly species have unlocked an evolutionary way to live longer  CNN
  2. Evolution of increased longevity and slowed ageing in a genus of tropical butterfly  Nature
  3. The Butterfly Longevity Diet  The Atlantic
  4. Butterflies may hold clues to longer lives  NPR
  5. Tropical butterfly defies aging, lives 348 days and reveals longevity clues  mezha.net

item.title: 'No one thought it was going to be possible.' A space telescope is falling out of space. This is NASA's daring plan to save it. - Space
item.description:
1. 'No one thought it was going to be possible.' A space telescope is falling out of space. This is NASA's daring plan to save it.  Space

  4. Northrop Grumman’s Pegasus Rocket to Power Unprecedented NASA Observatory Rescue  Northrop Grumman
  5. Inside One Startup’s Race To Rescue NASA’s Sinking Space Telescope  Forbes

item.title: Antarctica is offering 30 to 50 years' worth of warning on sea level rise, models suggest - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Antarctica is offering 30 to 50 years' worth of warning on sea level rise, models suggest  Phys.org
  2. Emergent decadal predictability in Antarctic contribution to sea-level rise  Nature
  3. Scientists say the West Antarctic Ice Sheet could collapse with very little additional warming and the four metres of sea level rise that would follow cannot be stopped once it begins  Space Daily
  4. Vanderford Glacier, Credit: Monash SAEF (IMAGE)  EurekAlert!

item.title: 'A completely different story': 300 million-year-old fossils reveal the first vertebrate land dwellers weren't what we thought, researchers claim - Live Science
item.description:
1. 'A completely different story': 300 million-year-old fossils reveal the first vertebrate land dwellers weren't what we thought, researchers claim  Live Science
  2. A Vietnam Veteran Collected Fossils for 66 Years. One, Mislabeled 'Baby Lamprey,' Made Paleontologists Reconsider How Vertebrates Moved From Water to Land  Smithsonian Magazine
  3. A textbook assumption about early land vertebrates may be wrong  Science News
  4. Earth’s First Land Animals May Never Have Been Amphibian-Like After All  Sci.News
  5. Ancient Crocodile-Like Babies Challenge What We Know About Tetrapod Evolution  Discover Magazine

item.title: In 1908, something exploded above the remote Siberian forest of Tunguska with enough force to flatten roughly 2,000 square kilometres of trees — and more than a century later, no impact crater has ever been found, because the object, probably a small aster - Space Daily
item.description: In 1908, something exploded above the remote Siberian forest of Tunguska with enough force to flatten roughly 2,000 square kilometres of trees — and more than a century later, no impact crater has ever been found, because the object, probably a small aster  Space Daily

item.title: HD 189733b looks deep blue from space, almost Earth-like at first glance — but its colour comes not from oceans, but from silicate particles in a scorching atmosphere where glass may rain sideways through winds of thousands of kilometres an hour. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. HD 189733b looks deep blue from space, almost Earth-like at first glance — but its colour comes not from oceans, but from silicate particles in a scorching atmosphere where glass may rain sideways through winds of thousands of kilometres an hour.  Space Daily
  2. Cotton-candy planets and metal rain: NASA finds some of the strangest worlds in space  The Times of India

item.title: A Greenland shark studied in 2016 was estimated to be nearly 400 years old, meaning it may have been born around the time Shakespeare’s era was ending — and, astonishingly, would not have reached sexual maturity for more than another century. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A Greenland shark studied in 2016 was estimated to be nearly 400 years old, meaning it may have been born around the time Shakespeare’s era was ending — and, astonishingly, would not have reached sexual maturity for more than another century.  Space Daily
  2. How Nuclear Weapons Helped Reveal That The Longest-living Vertebrate On Earth Can Reach A Whopping 400 Years  IFLScience
  3. Note on Emerging Science: Long-Lived Greenland Sharks  Living on Earth
  4. The longest-living animals on Earth: These remarkable creatures can survive for 200 years and some even longer  The Times of India
  5. "It doesn't reach sexual maturity until 150 years old and can be pregnant for a humongous 18 years." Meet the 10 longest living animals on the planet  BBC Wildlife Magazine

item.title: When a dead whale sinks to the seafloor, its 'whale fall' becomes an oasis feeding deep-sea life for decades — up to 190 species crowding a single skeleton, with bone-eating worms gnawing the bones for as long as a decade - Space Daily
item.description:
1. When a dead whale sinks to the seafloor, its 'whale fall' becomes an oasis feeding deep-sea life for decades — up to 190 species crowding a single skeleton, with bone-eating worms gnawing the bones for as long as a decade  Space Daily
  2. Bury me in the whale graveyard – let the weird starfish chew my bones | First Dog on the Moon  The Guardian
  3. World's Largest 'Whale Graveyard' Teems with Deep-Sea Life Including Species Unknown to Science  Good News Network
  4. Scientists Found 476 Dead Whales in One Ocean Trench  Popular Mechanics
  5. A 5-million-year-old whale graveyard  Deccan Herald

item.title: Humans May Soon Be Able to Regrow Body Parts—Including Fingers and Limbs—Thanks to a Groundbreaking Serum - Yahoo
item.description:
1. Humans May Soon Be Able to Regrow Body Parts—Including Fingers and Limbs—Thanks to a Groundbreaking Serum  Yahoo

  5. Scientists Discover Gene That One Day Could Help Humans Regrow Lost Limbs  ColombiaOne.com

item.description:

item.title: Enceladus, a tiny Saturnian moon only about 500 kilometres wide, is actively venting water vapour and ice grains into space from a salty ocean hidden beneath its crust — and modelling suggests that ocean may have remained warm and chemically active for - Space Daily

item.title: On 5 March 1982, the Soviet Venera 14 lander reached the surface of Venus, ejected the protective cap from its camera lens, photographed the surrounding terrain — and then lowered its mechanical soil-testing arm directly onto the discarded lens cap, returni - 19FortyFive

item.title: The reason no human being has walked on the Moon in more than half a century is not that humanity lost the capability to get there — it is that humanity lost the reason, in the moment the Cold War goal of beating the Soviet Union to the lunar surface had finall - Space Daily

item.title: An exoplanet with a daytime temperature hot enough to vaporize iron has methane on its nightside because of an atmospheric circulation that should not be able to exist at that heat - Space Daily
item.description:

  4. Asymmetric weather pattern reveals exoplanet’s true atmosphere  Physics World

item.title: In late 2023, Voyager 1 began sending unreadable data from interstellar space. NASA engineers eventually traced the fault to corrupted memory in one of the probe’s computers, rewrote part of its 46-year-old software, transmitted the fix across more than 24 bil - Space Daily

item.title: The tallest volcano in the solar system is so wide that from most places on its slopes, you would never see a dramatic summit rising ahead of you — Olympus Mons is broader than some countries, and its gentle flanks curve away with Mars long before the mou - Space Daily

item.title: The longest continuous spaceflight in history — 437 days aboard the Russian Mir space station, completed by cosmonaut Valeri Polyakov in 1995 — was specifically conducted to prove that a human being could survive a round-trip mission to Mars, in a piece - Space Daily
